13.play: Stir up the Athenian youth to merriments;

14.play: Awake the pert and nimble spirit of mirth;

16.play: The pale companion is not for our pomp.

17.play: Hippolyta, I woo'd thee with my sword,

18.play: And won thy love, doing thee injuries;

19.play: But I will wed thee in another key,

20.play: With pomp, with triumph and with revelling.

21.play: EGEUS: Happy be Theseus, our renowned duke!

23.play: EGEUS: Full of vexation come I, with complaint

24.play: Against my child, my daughter Hermia.

25.play: Stand forth, Demetrius. My noble lord,

26.play: This man hath my consent to marry her.

27.play: Stand forth, Lysander: and my gracious duke,

28.play: This man hath bewitch'd the bosom of my child;

29.play: Thou, thou, Lysander, thou hast given her rhymes,

30.play: And interchanged love-tokens with my child:

31.play: Thou hast by moonlight at her window sung,

32.play: With feigning voice verses of feigning love,

33.play: And stolen the impression of her fantasy

34.play: With bracelets of thy hair, rings, gawds, conceits,

36.play: OF STRONG PREVAILMENT IN UNHARDEN'D YOUTH:

37.play: With cunning hast thou filch'd my daughter's heart,

38.play: Turn'd her obedience, which is due to me,

39.play: TO STUBBORN HARSHNESS: and, my gracious duke,

40.play: Be it so she; will not here before your grace

42.play: I beg the ancient privilege of Athens,

43.play: As she is mine, I may dispose of her:

44.play: Which shall be either to this gentleman

45.play: Or to her death, according to our law

47.play: THESEUS: What say you, Hermia? be advised fair maid:

48.play: To you your father should be as a god;

49.play: One that composed your beauties, yea, and one

50.play: To whom you are but as a form in wax

51.play: By him imprinted and within his power

52.play: To leave the figure or disfigure it.

56.play: But in this kind, wanting your father's voice,

57.play: The other must be held the worthier.

58.play: HERMIA: I would my father look'd but with my eyes.

59.play: THESEUS: Rather your eyes must with his judgment look.

60.play: HERMIA: I do entreat your grace to pardon me.

61.play: I know not by what power I am made bold,

62.play: Nor how it may concern my modesty,

63.play: In such a presence here to plead my thoughts;

64.play: But I beseech your grace that I may know

65.play: The worst that may befall me in this case,

66.play: If I refuse to wed Demetrius.

67.play: THESEUS: Either to die the death or to abjure

68.play: For ever the society of men.

69.play: Therefore, fair Hermia, question your desires;

70.play: Know of your youth, examine well your blood,

71.play: Whether, if you yield not to your father's choice,

72.play: You can endure the livery of a nun,

73.play: For aye to be in shady cloister mew'd,

74.play: To live a barren sister all your life,

75.play: Chanting faint hymns to the cold fruitless moon.

76.play: Thrice-blessed they that master so their blood,

78.play: But earthlier happy is the rose distill'd,

79.play: Than that which withering on the virgin thorn

80.play: Grows, lives and dies in single blessedness.

81.play: HERMIA: So will I grow, so live, so die, my lord,

82.play: Ere I will my virgin patent up

83.play: Unto his lordship, whose unwished yoke

84.play: My soul consents not to give sovereignty.

85.play: THESEUS: Take time to pause; and, by the next new moon--

86.play: The sealing-day betwixt my love and me,

88.play: Upon that day either prepare to die

89.play: For disobedience to your father's will,

90.play: Or else to wed Demetrius, as he would;

91.play: Or on Diana's altar to protest

92.play: For aye austerity and single life.

93.play: DEMETRIUS: Relent, sweet Hermia: and, Lysander, yield

94.play: Thy crazed title to my certain right.

95.play: LYSANDER: You have her father's love, Demetrius;

96.play: LET ME HAVE HERMIA'S: do you marry him.

97.play: EGEUS: Scornful Lysander! true, he hath my love,

98.play: And what is mine my love shall render him.

99.play: And she is mine, and all my right of her

101.play: LYSANDER: I am, my lord, as well derived as he,

102.play: As well possess'd; my love is more than his;

103.play: My fortunes every way as fairly rank'd,

104.play: If not with vantage, as Demetrius';

105.play: And, which is more than all these boasts can be,

106.play: I AM BELOVED OF BEAUTEOUS HERMIA:

107.play: Why should not I then prosecute my right?

108.play: Demetrius, I'll avouch it to his head,

109.play: Made love to Nedar's daughter, Helena,

110.play: And won her soul; and she, sweet lady, dotes,

112.play: Upon this spotted and inconstant man.

113.play: THESEUS: I must confess that I have heard so much,

114.play: And with Demetrius thought to have spoke thereof;

116.play: My mind did lose it. But, Demetrius, come;

117.play: And come, Egeus; you shall go with me,

118.play: I have some private schooling for you both.

119.play: For you, fair Hermia, look you arm yourself

120.play: To fit your fancies to your father's will;

121.play: Or else the law of Athens yields you up--

122.play: Which by no means we may extenuate--

123.play: To death, or to a vow of single life.

124.play: Come, my Hippolyta: what cheer, my love?

126.play: I must employ you in some business

127.play: Against our nuptial and confer with you

128.play: Of something nearly that concerns yourselves.

129.play: EGEUS: With duty and desire we follow you.

130.play: LYSANDER: How now, my love! why is your cheek so pale?

131.play: How chance the roses there do fade so fast?

132.play: HERMIA: Belike for want of rain, which I could well

133.play: Beteem them from the tempest of my eyes.

134.play: LYSANDER: Ay me! for aught that I could ever read,

135.play: Could ever hear by tale or history,

136.play: The course of true love never did run smooth;

137.play: But, either it was different in blood,--

138.play: HERMIA: O cross! too high to be enthrall'd to low.

139.play: LYSANDER: Or else misgraffed in respect of years,--

140.play: HERMIA: O spite! too old to be engaged to young.

141.play: LYSANDER: Or else it stood upon the choice of friends,--

142.play: HERMIA: O hell! to choose love by another's eyes.

143.play: LYSANDER: Or, if there were a sympathy in choice,

144.play: War, death, or sickness did lay siege to it,

145.play: Making it momentany as a sound,

146.play: Swift as a shadow, short as any dream;

147.play: Brief as the lightning in the collied night,

148.play: That, in a spleen, unfolds both heaven and earth,

149.play: And ere a man hath power to say 'Behold!'

150.play: THE JAWS OF DARKNESS DO DEVOUR IT UP:

151.play: So quick bright things come to confusion.

152.play: HERMIA: If then true lovers have been ever cross'd,

153.play: IT STANDS AS AN EDICT IN DESTINY:

154.play: Then let us teach our trial patience,

155.play: Because it is a customary cross,

156.play: As due to love as thoughts and dreams and sighs,

157.play: Wishes and tears, poor fancy's followers.

158.play: LYSANDER: A good persuasion: therefore, hear me, Hermia.

159.play: I have a widow aunt, a dowager

160.play: Of great revenue, and she hath no child:

161.play: From Athens is her house remote seven leagues;

162.play: And she respects me as her only son.

163.play: There, gentle Hermia, may I marry thee;

164.play: And to that place the sharp Athenian law

165.play: Cannot pursue us. If thou lovest me then,

166.play: Steal forth thy father's house to-morrow night;

167.play: And in the wood, a league without the town,

168.play: Where I did meet thee once with Helena,

169.play: To do observance to a morn of May,

170.play: There will I stay for thee.

172.play: I swear to thee, by Cupid's strongest bow,

173.play: By his best arrow with the golden head,

174.play: By the simplicity of Venus' doves,

175.play: By that which knitteth souls and prospers loves,

176.play: And by that fire which burn'd the Carthage queen,

177.play: When the false Troyan under sail was seen,

178.play: By all the vows that ever men have broke,

179.play: In number more than ever women spoke,

180.play: In that same place thou hast appointed me,

181.play: To-morrow truly will I meet with thee.

182.play: LYSANDER: Keep promise, love. Look, here comes Helena.

183.play: HERMIA: God speed fair Helena! whither away?

184.play: HELENA: Call you me fair? that fair again unsay.

185.play: DEMETRIUS LOVES YOUR FAIR: O happy fair!

186.play: Your eyes are lode-stars; and your tongue's sweet air

187.play: More tuneable than lark to shepherd's ear,

188.play: When wheat is green, when hawthorn buds appear.

189.play: SICKNESS IS CATCHING: O, were favour so,

190.play: Yours would I catch, fair Hermia, ere I go;

191.play: My ear should catch your voice, my eye your eye,

192.play: My tongue should catch your tongue's sweet melody.

193.play: Were the world mine, Demetrius being bated,

194.play: The rest I'd give to be to you translated.

195.play: O, teach me how you look, and with what art

196.play: You sway the motion of Demetrius' heart.

197.play: HERMIA: I frown upon him, yet he loves me still.

198.play: HELENA: O that your frowns would teach my smiles such skill!

199.play: HERMIA: I give him curses, yet he gives me love.

200.play: HELENA: O that my prayers could such affection move!

201.play: HERMIA: The more I hate, the more he follows me.

202.play: HELENA: The more I love, the more he hateth me.

203.play: HERMIA: His folly, Helena, is no fault of mine.

204.play: HELENA: None, but your beauty: would that fault were mine!

205.play: HERMIA: Take comfort: he no more shall see my face;

206.play: Lysander and myself will fly this place.

207.play: Before the time I did Lysander see,

208.play: SEEM'D ATHENS AS A PARADISE TO ME:

209.play: O, then, what graces in my love do dwell,

210.play: That he hath turn'd a heaven unto a hell!

212.play: To-morrow night, when Phoebe doth behold

213.play: Her silver visage in the watery glass,

214.play: Decking with liquid pearl the bladed grass,

215.play: A time that lovers' flights doth still conceal,

217.play: HERMIA: And in the wood, where often you and I

219.play: Emptying our bosoms of their counsel sweet,

226.play: From lovers' food till morrow deep midnight.

236.play: Things base and vile, folding no quantity,

255.play: To have his sight thither and back again.

371.play: Every one lets forth his sprite,

372.play: In the church-way paths to glide:

373.play: And we fairies, that do run

375.play: From the presence of the sun,

377.play: NOW ARE FROLIC: not a mouse

379.play: I am sent with broom before,

380.play: To sweep the dust behind the door.

381.play: OBERON: Through the house give gathering light,

382.play: BY THE DEAD AND DROWSY FIRE:

384.play: Hop as light as bird from brier;

387.play: TITANIA: First, rehearse your song by rote

388.play: TO EACH WORD A WARBLING NOTE:

389.play: Hand in hand, with fairy grace,

390.play: Will we sing, and bless this place.

391.play: OBERON: Now, until the break of day,

392.play: Through this house each fairy stray.

393.play: To the best bride-bed will we,

394.play: Which by us shall blessed be;

397.play: So shall all the couples three

399.play: And the blots of Nature's hand

400.play: Shall not in their issue stand;

401.play: Never mole, hare lip, nor scar,

402.play: Nor mark prodigious, such as are

408.play: Through this palace, with sweet peace;

409.play: And the owner of it blest

412.play: Meet me all by break of day.
